content:
2 min read
7 hours ago

--

In NgRx SignalStore, deep signals for nested objects are evaluated and created lazily — only when the property is first accessed. By default, properties must be defined in the initial state so the store’s reactivity system can recognize and wrap them.
I am referring to Deep Signals in Angular Signals and NgRx Signal State.
Traditional Object Access
const person = {
name: 'John',
address: {
street: '123 Main St'
}
};Angular doesn’t track changes at every nested property level.
Signal State
readonly person = signalState<Person>({
id: 1,
name: 'John Doe',
address: {
street: '123 Main St',
city: 'Anytown',
state: 'CA'
}
});Internally Angular creates signals.
But it does not immediately create:
person.address
person.address.street
person.address.city
person.address.statesignals.
What Happens?
Initially:
person signal
|
+-- objectOnly root signal exists.
First Access
When you do:
this.person.address.streetAngular creates:
person
└── address
└── streetsignal lazily.
Only now does Angular start tracking street.
Why?
Imagine:
User
├── Profile
├── Address
├── Orders
├── Preferences
├── Settings
├── Notificationswith thousands of properties.
Creating signals for everything upfront would:

• Consume memory

• Increase initialization time

• Create unnecessary dependencies
Lazy creation means:
✅ Better memory usage
✅ Faster startup
✅ Smaller reactive graph
Example
export class AppComponent { readonly person = signalState<Person>({
id: 1,
name: 'John Doe',
address: {
street: '123 Main St',
city: 'Anytown',
state: 'CA'
}
});
readonly personStreet = this.person.address.street;
}
Person Model
export interface Person {
readonly id: number;
readonly name: string;
readonly address: Address;
}Address Model
export interface Address {
readonly street: string;
readonly city: string;
readonly state: string;
}What’s Happening?
this.person.address.streetis NOT a string.
It is actually:
Signal<string>Think of it as:
const streetSignal = signal('123 Main St');Reading Value
console.log(this.personStreet());Output:
123 Main StNotice:
personStreet()because signals are functions.
Updating Value
patchState(this.person, {
address: {
street: '456 New Street'
}
});Now:
personStreet()automatically returns:
456 New Streetwithout manual subscriptions.
Deep Signal Visualization
person (Signal<Person>)
│
├── id (Signal<number>)
│
├── name (Signal<string>)
│
└── address
│
├── street (Signal<string>)
│
├── city (Signal<string>)
│
└── state (Signal<string>)Each nested property becomes reactive.
